coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots promoting surface defect and interphase high anion concentration for sodium-ion battery carbon anodes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Yue, Liang
Ding, Haifeng
Xiong, Zhiyong
Bai, Lixin
Xu, Maowen
Qi, Yuruo
coredata.dc:description: The interphase chemistry of electrode/electrolyte significantly affects the electrochemical performance. This investigation explores the solid-liquid interphase alteration of an economical asphalt-derived carbon by incorporating multifunctional carbon dots, aiming to clarify the impact of interfacial properties on sodium-ion storage mechanisms. Enhanced by high density defects and carbonyl groups, the capacity, primarily within the sloping discharge, rise from 247 to 361 mA h g−1 in non-aqueous sodium ion batteries. Integrating experimental and computational methods reveals a marked improvement in PF6 - anions absorption at the interface, refining the solvation structure and concentrating these anions. Consequently, a NaF/Na2CO3-dominated, inorganic-rich solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) forms, attributed to the swift PF6 - anion dissociation facilitated by the electrode's rapid electron mobility. This effect promotes sodium ion diffusion and accelerates the kinetics of sodium (de)intercalation. Beyond elucidating interfacial property contributions to the sodium storage in disordered carbon, this study highlights the genesis of irreversible capacity and draws connections between SEI's inorganic components and Coulombic efficiency. The insights offered furnish a pioneering perspective for engineering high-performance carbon anodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Electrode/electrolyte interphase

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the support from Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2023NSFSC1129), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22005251, No. 22179109), Central University Research Funding Talent Introduction Program (NO. SWU-KR22002).

We appreciate the support from Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2023NSFSC1129), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22005251, No. 22179109), Central University Research Funding Talent Introduction Program (NO. SWU-KR22002). Yi Zhang. Liang Yue. and Yuruo Qi. conceived and designed the experiment. Yi Zhang. prepared samples and carried out the main experiments. Yi Zhang. and Yue Liang. examined the electrochemical performance. Yandong Ma. Jing Zhao. Haifeng Ding. Zhiyong Xiong. Lixin Bai. and Juan Chen. assisted the investigation. Yi Zhang. Yuruo Qi. and Maowen Xu. wrote and revised the paper. All authors have read and approved the final paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structure characterization of CDAC and AC. (a) Schematic illustration of the electrolyte-electrode interphase engineering with carbon dots. (b-d) SEM, TEM and HRTEM images of AC. (e-g) SEM, TEM and HRTEM images of CDAC.

Physical characterization of CDAC and AC. (a) TGA curves. (b) N2 adsorption/desorption measurements. (c) XRD patterns. (d) Raman spectra. (e) Comparison of d002, Lc, R, ID/IG and La. (f) Elemental analysis. (g) Fitting results of high-resolution O1s XPS spectra. (h) FTIR spectra. (i) Electronic resistivity.

SEI characterization of CDAC and AC. (a) Survey XPS spectra. (b-c) High-resolution F1s XPS spectra of CDAC and AC before and after Ar+ etching. (d-e) High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of CDAC and AC before and after Ar+ etching. (f) Percentage of F species in the SEI of CDAC and AC. (g) Ratio of inorganic and organic composition. (h-k) TEM images of (h-i) CDAC and (j-k) AC after 10 cycles.

DFT calculation and electronic structure characterization of CDAC and AC. (a) Adsorption energies of NaPF6 on C-C, CO and C-N bonds. (b) Dissociation barriers of NaPF6 on C-N, C-C and CO. (c-d) Work function and valence-band maximum of CDAC and AC fitted from Ultraviolate Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS). (e) Schematic diagram of the reduction energies of electrolytes at surface of CDAC and AC.

Electrochemical characterization of CDAC and AC. (a) First cycle of GCD curves in the potential range of 0–2.5 V at 0.1 C (30 mA g−1). (b) Rate performance. (c) Cycle performance of half cells at 0.1 C. (d-e) Operando Raman spectra of CDAC d) and AC e). (f) Intensity ratio of D and G peaks at different voltages. (g) GCD curves of full cells. (h) Cycling performance of full cells at 0.5 C.

Kinetic analysis of the sodium storage reaction of CDAC and AC. (a) CV curves. (b-c) Na+ diffusion coefficients during the discharge process calculated from GITT for the (b) first cycle, (c) second cycle. (d-e) Fitted impedances during first cycle from Nyquist plots at varied voltages. (f) In situ EIS measurements at different temperatures. (g-h) Arrhenius curves and calculated activation energies for R SEI and R ct from Nyquist plots in (f).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots promoting surface defect and interphase high anion concentration for sodium-ion battery carbon anodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Battery Materials and Technologies, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Battery Materials and Technologies, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Faculty of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yibin Academy of Southwest University, Yibin 644000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yibin Academy of Southwest University, Yibin 644000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Battery Materials and Technologies, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Battery Materials and Technologies, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interphase chemistry of electrode/electrolyte significantly affects the electrochemical performance. This investigation explores the solid-liquid interphase alteration of an economical asphalt-derived carbon by incorporating multifunctional carbon dots, aiming to clarify the impact of interfacial properties on sodium-ion storage mechanisms. Enhanced by high density defects and carbonyl groups, the capacity, primarily within the sloping discharge, rise from 247 to 361 mA h g−1 in non-aqueous sodium ion batteries. Integrating experimental and computational methods reveals a marked improvement in PF6 - anions absorption at the interface, refining the solvation structure and concentrating these anions. Consequently, a NaF/Na2CO3-dominated, inorganic-rich solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) forms, attributed to the swift PF6 - anion dissociation facilitated by the electrode's rapid electron mobility. This effect promotes sodium ion diffusion and accelerates the kinetics of sodium (de)intercalation. Beyond elucidating interfacial property contributions to the sodium storage in disordered carbon, this study highlights the genesis of irreversible capacity and draws connections between SEI's inorganic components and Coulombic efficiency. The insights offered furnish a pioneering perspective for engineering high-performance carbon anodes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This investigation explores the solid-liquid interphase alteration of an economical asphalt-derived carbon by incorporating multifunctional carbon dots, aiming to clarify the impact of interfacial properties on sodium-ion storage mechanisms. Enhanced by high density defects and carbonyl groups, the capacity, primarily within the sloping discharge, rise from 247 to 361 mA h g-1 in non-aqueous sodium ion batteries. Integrating experimental and computational methods reveals a marked improvement in PF6- anions absorption at the interface, refining the solvation structure and concentrating these anions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are extensively explored as a supplement for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in recent years, due to the abundant resources and even distribution of sodium in the world. In contrast to the rapid development of cathodes, the progress of anodes is a bit sluggish. Disordered carbons have been recognized as the most ideal type of anodes for SIBs. However, the specific capacity, initial Coulombic efficiency and rate performance of disordered carbons cannot meet the commercialization requirements of SIBs. Nowadays, many strategies have been proposed to improve the electrochemical performance of disordered carbons. For example, increasing the interlayer spacing to enhance the insertion ability of sodium ions [1], modulating the pore structure to accommodate more metallic clusters [2,3], changing the degree of graphitization and defect concentration to obtain higher sodium affinity [4], doping heteroatoms to increase conductivity and provide more active sites, have been proved to be useful [5]. These methods are all devoted to regulating the microstructure inside the bulk phase, with the purpose of improving the electrochemical performance and probing the sodium storage mechanism.

In addition to bulk structural features, the electrode/electrolyte interphase properties would greatly affect the electrochemical performance as well. For example, Forsyth et al. [6] reported that the different interfacial metal-anion-solvent composition, which correlates with the dielectric properties of electrode materials, renders different initial cycling conditions. In addition, fast bulk Na+ transportation and robust inorganic-rich solid state interphase (SEI) were demonstrated to be helpful in achieving a high-rate and low-temperature-applicable hard carbon [7]. These findings are informative and groundbreaking, but further efforts are necessary to adequately address the importance of electrode/electrolyte interphase. Therefore, it is worthwhile to explore more rational interphases and investigate their effectiveness and underlying mechanisms in performance enhancement.

Herein, the electrode-electrolyte interphase of asphalt-derived carbon was elaborately modulated by carbon dots. The unsaturated bonds and functional groups in carbon dots significantly boost the surface defect degree of the carbon electrode [8], thereby enhancing the capacity in the slope segment. Theoretical calculations and experimental results suggest that the inclusion of carbonyl functional groups (CO) could amplify the adsorption of PF6 - anions on the electrode interphase, thus achieving a high localized concentration of anions at the solid-liquid interface. Moreover, a lower electron escape energy barrier for the modified material facilitates the decomposition of PF6 - on the electrode surface, ultimately leading to an improved content of NaF and Na2CO3 in SEI ( Fig. 1 a). The synergistic effect between these inorganic salts can help enable a higher concentration of ionic carriers, finally enabling more rapid ionic diffusion and reduced impedance. Additionally, some understandings regarding the origin of irreversible capacity as well as the correlation between the inorganic composition in SEI and the Coulombic efficiency are acquired in this work. This study not only offers a design methodology for an advanced SEI layer, but also provides some references for further exploration on interfacial properties of hard carbons.

The original substrate (AC) shows a smooth and flat morphology (Fig. 1 b), which is gradually covered by carbon dots (Fig. 1 e). The generation of carbon dots is affirmed by the fluorescence characteristic presented under purple lights (Fig. S1). It is found that the number and size of carbon dots are positively correlated with the reaction time (Fig. S2). Notably, when the reaction time is 5 hours, it exhibits the highest uniformity and density with little impurities or aggregates. Therefore, the product obtained at 5 h is used for following discussions, denoted as CDAC. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 1 f and Fig. S4 reveal the uniform anchor of carbon dots on the surface, rather than embedding in the bulk phase. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1 g suggests that carbon dots on the surface are well crystallized (featured with obvious lattice stripes and an interlayer spacing of 0.24 nm), while the bulk phase is highly disordered (characterized by randomly-oriented short carbon layers and a larger interlayer spacing of 0.37 nm).

The amount of carbon dots in CDAC was first quantified by Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) test ( Fig. 2 a). A distinct two-stage process is recorded for CDs. The loss of water molecules and other small volatiles (∼ 30%) occurs in the temperature range of 30–172 °C, while the condensation carbonization (50%) happens exceeding 172 °C [9,10]. For AC, only a small mass loss (2.3%) is observed in the temperature range of 30–800 °C, which may be associated with the evaporation of residual N/H/O heteroatoms. However, when carbon dots are introduced, the mass loss reaches 25.4%. Thus, the mass fraction of carbon dots in CDAC is roughly calculated to be 8%. It is found that decreased specific surface area (Fig. 2 b) and pore volume (Fig. S6) are measured when carbon dots were introduced.

Fig. 2 c presents the XRD patterns of CDAC and AC. Both samples show broad peaks at around 25.2 and 43.2°, which are ascribed to the crystallographic planes of (002) and (100) in disordered carbons. The (002) diffraction peak of CDAC stays almost at the same position with that of AC and the interlayer spacing (d002) of both samples is almost identical (0.352 nm and 0.345 nm, respectively) according to the Bragg equation. Moreover, Lc and R values which can characterize the stacking of carbon sheets along C-axis [11] are approximately the same (Fig. 2 e). The identical XRD patterns and tiny variation in these calculated parameters imply that the incorporation of carbon dots does not alter the internal bulk phase structure, in accordant with the foregoing SEM/TEM characterization.

Different from the above XRD results, Raman spectra (Fig. 2 d) show that the ID/IG value of CDAC (2.59) is larger than AC (2.20). The corresponding coherence length of graphitic domains along the lateral ab planes (La) calculated by the method proposed by Cancado [12] becomes smaller (7.42 nm for CDAC vs.8.74 nm for AC). The obvious increase of ID/IG together with the reduction of La indicate that the defect concentration in CDAC is significantly enhanced, which may be related to the large number of defects from carbon dots [13–15].

To clarify the defects and heteroatoms species, elemental analysis (EA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were carried out. As listed by elemental analysis in Fig. 2 f, the pristine carbon (AC) derived from the low-temperature carbonized asphalt contains small amounts of N (0.32 wt%), H (1.01 wt%) and O (7.07 wt%). After the introduction of carbon dots, the proportion of N and O is largely increased to 2.62 wt% and 14.66 wt%, consistent with the electronic diffraction spectroscopy (EDS) mapping images in Fig. S3. The O1s XPS spectra of these two samples are fitted to two bands at 533.0 and 532.2 eV, which are attributed to ether (-C-O-) and carbonyl (-CO), respectively. As listed in Table S1, the pristine substrate AC consists of 41.3% C-O and 58.7% CO species, while the CO species in CDAC significantly increase to 72.6%. This is well substantiated by the FTIR data in Fig. 2 h, where CDAC shows higher relative intensity of CO/C-O than AC. This indicates that the introduction of carbon dots can induce more CO groups, which has been reported to be favorable for sodium storage in carbon anodes [16]. The high resolution N1s spectra are displayed in Fig. S9. The CDAC is characterized by dominant graphitic nitrogen which has been reported to boost the electronic conductivity [17]. This is supported by the powder resistivity of CDAC and AC in Fig. 2 i. Meanwhile, the existence of pyridinic and pyrrole nitrogen can enhance the affinity between carbon anodes and electrolytes, which may achieve optimization of the electrode-electrolyte interphase. The improved affinity is verified by a better wettability of the electrode, as illustrated in Fig. S8. Based on the above analyses, the CDAC exhibits higher defects concentrations as well as more N/O species especially CO groups and graphitic nitrogen, but few structural differences in bulk phase.

Considering that interfacial features would greatly influence electrochemical performance, we further examined the effects of CDs on interfacial chemistry and structure. Fig. 3 a shows the survey XPS spectra of SEI at different depths before and after Ar+ etching. The most significant feature is that the F peaks in CDAC is more intensive than AC, which means more F contents in the SEI of CDAC. Specifically, the F content in SEI of CDAC is around 10% (9.96% before etching, 11.52% after etching), while it only occupies ∼ 2% in AC (2.12% before etching, 1.97% after etching). As F in the SEI can only come from the electrolytic salt NaPF6, the much more F content in the case of CDAC suggests that CDs on the surface of CDAC facilitate the decomposition of electrolytic salt NaPF6.

Usually, there are two primary mechanisms for the breakdown of NaPF6 [23]. One is reacted with trace amounts of water molecules (equ. (1) and (2)), which occurs more commonly but slowly. Another route is reacted with Lewis acids (equ. (3) and (4)), for instance, RCO3 that is already presented in the SEI. The possible fluorine compounds detected on the electrode surface include the inorganic salt NaF and the organic substance (RF), corresponding to Na-F and C-F, respectively.

To figure out the specific F species in SEI, high-resolution F1s XPS spectra were examined. As shown in Fig. 3 b-c, both samples reveal analogous peak profiles, fitting with C-F, P-F and Na-F bonds located at 689.1 eV, 686.9 eV and 683.8 eV respectively. The outer SEI layer (before etching) of both samples is mostly composed of P-F such as NaxPFy and/or NaxPOyF [18]. Besides, F-containing organic spices (C-F groups) also occupy a small part of the outer SEI, which could prevent the SEI from dissolving in electrolyte [19]. The inner SEI layer (after etching) mainly consists of NaF, with almost no organic components. Although the similar SEI configuration and constituent for two samples, a prominent distinction between them is the tremendously increased NaF content in CDAC. In detail, the Na-F specie in internal SEI takes up 7.31% of all elements for CDAC, while it is only 1.53% for AC.

The C1s XPS spectra of CDAC and AC shown in Fig. 3 d-e are deconvoluted into six peaks located at 283.4 eV (NaxHC), 284.8 eV (C-C), 286.5 eV (C-O), 287.9 eV (O-CO), 289.7 eV (CO) and 290.7 eV (C-F), which could be ascribed to sodiated carbon, unsodiated carbon, organic components (RCH2ONa, ROCO2Na, RF) and inorganic components (Na2CO3 and Na2O), respectively [20]. Two discernable features can be observed. One is the C-C and NaxHC peaks of CDAC is intensified after etching while these two species in AC remain almost the same as those before etching, indicating a thinner SEI in CDAC. The second feature is the increased content of CO and O-CO compared to C-O, indicating that Na2CO3 appears predominantly in the inner SEI layer of CDAC. O1s spectra in Fig. S10 agree well with the above results. Consequently, a quantitative analysis of the component distribution was also extracted from F1s and C1s spectra, as shown in Table S2 and Fig. 3 g. The results reveal that the inorganic substance in CDAC takes a larger percentage (19.1% before etching, 25.8% after etching) compared with AC (9.2% before etching, 12.5% after etching).

Furthermore, HRTEM (Fig. 3 h-k) was employed to characterize the specific microstructure of SEI. Accordant with the deduction obtained from C1s spectra, CDAC shows a thinner SEI with an average thickness of 7.7 nm (17.1 nm for AC). Besides, a larger proportion of inorganic salts with well-defined crystal lattices, including NaF, Na2CO3 and Na2O, is observed in CDAC. By contrast, for the SEI of AC, the amorphous region is distributed nearly in the whole SEI and inorganic components only account for a small part. Taken together the XPS and TEM results, it can be concluded that the introduction of CDs does not bring new species, but could result in a thinner and inorganic-rich SEI, containing more NaF, Na2CO3 and Na2O species. Additionally, we further examined the SEI of CDAC and AC when cycled at a high temperature of 45 °C. HRTEM images in Fig. S17 show that CDAC has thinner SEI, more inorganic components than AC at 45 °C, which is consistent with the result at 25 °C (Fig. 3 h-j). This results once again demonstrate that the SEI is not the main reason for its lower ICE, but the defects in CDAC should take the responsibility.

The aforementioned XPS and HRTEM analysis verify the noteworthy rise in NaF and Na2CO3 content within the SEI of CDAC. It is acknowledged that NaF is originated from the decomposition of NaPF6; whereas, Na2CO3 mainly comes from the combination of sodium ions with the degradation products of organic solvents [21,22]. These results suggest that the distinct interphase properties of CDAC modulate the decomposition process of electrolyte salts and solvents, leading to the formation of SEI containing higher levels of NaF and Na2CO3. To gain further insight on the function of CDs in tailoring SEI, a theoretical investigation based on Density Functional Theory (DFT) was conducted. Since our foregoing observations had shown that carbon dots mainly result in the introduction of CO and C-N groups, it is reasonable to choose C-C, CO and C-N bonds for calculation representatively. The calculated adsorption energies of NaPF6 on C-C, CO and C-N bonds were depicted in Fig. 4 a. The positive adsorption energy between C-C and NaPF6 (0.245 eV) suggests that the adsorption of NaPF6 on AC is endothermic and unfavorable. On the other hand, the adsorption energy of NaPF6 on CO and C-N are −0.6 and −0.286 eV, respectively, which suggests that CO and C-N groups in CDAC can facilitate the adsorption of NaPF6. In addition, the dissociation ability of NaPF6 into NaF was estimated by the calculation of transition state (Fig. 4 b). The dissociation energy of NaPF6 on C-C, C-N and CO are 2.43, 2.45 and 2.16 eV, respectively. Apparently, CO provides the lowest energy barrier for the dissociation of NaPF6. These findings can support the opinion that the carbon dots-modified material substantially facilitates the adsorption and dissociation of NaPF6, thus leading to higher NaF content in the SEI layer [23].

Furthermore, the valence band structure and molecular energy level orbit of both samples were measured by Ultraviolate Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS). As shown in Fig. S11, the Fermi level of CDAC and AC are determined at 4.14 and 4.16 eV, respectively. It is found that the introduction of carbon dots renders the reduction of work function from 4.78 eV (AC) to 4.63 eV (CDAC) (Fig. 4 c-d) [24]. A lower work function means the minimum energy required for an electron to immediately escape from the surface of a solid is smaller in CDAC, compared with AC. Meanwhile, the valence-band maximum is also lifted from 2.89 eV (AC) to 3.18 eV (CDAC). When the conduction-band minimum remains the same, the rise of valence-band maximum makes it easier for electrons to jump from the valence band to the conduction band and thus accelerates the movement of electrons in the bulk phase of materials [25], in line with the improved electronic conductivity shown in Fig. 2 i. SEI is formed by the reduction of electrolyte at the surface of anode, involving the transfer of electrons from the electrode to the electrolyte [26]. Therefore, the incorporation of carbon dots, which improves electron mobility both interiorly within the bulk phase and exteriorly from the surface, can promote the release of electrons from the electrode to the electrolyte and thus boost the reduction of electrolyte at the interphase, finally reaching a SEI contains higher content of Na2CO3 (Fig. 4 e).

To understand the effect of carbon dots as well as the varied interphase feature on the electrochemical performance, CDAC, AC and CDs were first investigated with respect to sodium storage in half cells. Three samples present slope-dominated profiles, as seen from the galvanostatic discharge/charge curves in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S12. The CDs show a high reversible capacity of 353 mA h g−1 but low initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 42% poor cycling stability. The initial reversible capacity of CDAC (361 mA h g−1) is remarkably larger than that of AC (247.5 mA h g−1). It is worth noting that at a high rate of 10 C, CDAC still maintains a specific capacity of 107 mA h g−1, while the capacity of AC is almost negligible (Fig. 5 b). Importantly, the capacity can still keep at 268 mA h g−1 even at a rather high mass loading of 6.2 mg cm−2 (Fig. S13 and Table S3), further substantiating its enhanced reaction kinetics. In addition, although the capacity decreases clearly with increasing mass loading, the shape of GCD curves remains similar.

Operando Raman spectra were collected to probe changes in the chemical states and clarify possible sodium storage mechanism during the (de)sodiation process (Fig. 5 d-e). D peaks near 1300 cm−1 represent the lattice defects of C atoms, and G peaks near 1580 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of sp2-hybridized C atoms [27]. As the discharge proceeds, D-bands gradually fade away and vanish at around 0.9 V, while the intensity/position of G-bands are identical with the initial status. It has been reported that the breathing vibration of sodium ions absorbed at defect sites causes the weakening/disappearance of D-bands [28]. Additionally, the unchanged G-bands signifies the chemical state of C-C bonds maintains constant during the sodiation process [29]. Therefore, it is concluded that the sodium storage capacity of both samples is predominantly contributed by occurrence of sodium ions on defects. To clarify the difference in electrochemical process between these two samples and figure out the possible reason for the increased capacity of CDAC, the evolution of ID/IG during the sodiation process were further compared in Fig. 5 f. Both samples show decreased ID/IG values, further substantiate the sodium storage mechanism proved above. However, the ID/IG values of CDAC decrease more sharply than those of AC, demonstrating more defects provide the accessorial capacity of sodium storage in CDAC.

The initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) of CDAC and AC are 66% and 74%, respectively. Generally, the formation of SEI on the surface of carbons and the irreversible bonding of sodium ions in the bulk phase of carbons are two main reasons for the capacity loss in the first cycle. Apparently, the specific surface area (as discussed in the foregoing section, Fig. 2 e) as well as the SEI thickness (shown by Fig. 3 h) of CDAC is greatly decreased, whereas the irreversible capacity of CDAC is increased. Therefore, the irreversible bonding of sodium ions with defects and/or functional groups should be responsible for the large irreversible capacity of CDAC. In the cycling test performed at 30 mA g−1 (Fig. 5 c), both samples show moderate capacity retentions after 100 cycles (83% for AC, and 81% for CDAC).

Generally, inorganic components in SEI, such as NaF and Na2O, are believed to be favorable for electrochemical stability [30]. However, CDAC with more inorganic components in its SEI shows inferior ICE and cycling stability. Thus, we draw the conclusion that sodium storage behavior correlated with defects and/or functional groups tends to show low reversibility, although it is expected to increase the specific capacity. This is also inferred by a recent work, in which the authors demonstrated hard carbon with reduced residual oxygen atoms/defects exhibits remarkably improved initial Coulombic efficiency and cycling stability [31].

Furtherly, full cells based on the Na3(VOPO4)2F cathode were assembled and their electrochemical performance were evaluated in both the EC/DMC-based electrolyte and the DME-based electrolyte. The electrochemical performance of the Na3(VOPO4)2F cathode in half cells with both electrolytes were shown in Fig. S15. Combined with the slope-dominated CDAC anode, the voltage plateaus of full cells are no longer visible (Fig. 5 g and Fig. S16). Moreover, neither full cells in the EC/DMC-based electrolyte nor the DME-based electrolyte exhibit an enhanced cycling stability. Specifically, even in DME-based electrolyte, a significant degradation occurs in about 70 cycles (Fig. 5 h). Considering that ester and ether electrolytes lead to distinctive SEI, the less-improved cycling stability may lend a further support that the moderate Coulombic efficiency and cycling performance are not mainly derived from the SEI formation, but rather from bulk phase defects. This will be further studied in our future work.

Based on the preceding SEI and electrochemical analysis, it is clear that the function of carbon dots can be summarized as the following two points. On the electrode side, an abundance of defects/functional groups brings high capacity but lowers cycling stability. The other one is that the functional groups on carbon dots can facilitate the decomposition of anions, resulting in the creation of an inorganic-rich SEI comprising NaF and Na2CO3 predominantly [18,31–33]. Even though delivering poor sodium diffusivities (Na2CO3 ∼ 6×10−34 cm2 s−1 and NaF ∼ 2×10−49 cm2 s−1), the synergistic effect between these inorganic salts may generate higher ionic carrier concentration at nanoscale size, thus the thinner inorganic-rich SEI in CDAC could provide rapid transport of Na+ and electronic insulation [34–38]. Therefore, this occurrence in interfacial kinetics enhancement was further investigated.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, substantial differences in the onset potential and peak shape of SEI formation are observed between CDAC and AC. For CDAC, a prominent SEI peak is observed at a voltage of 0.6 V during the initial discharge process, while the beginning of SEI formation for AC is recorded at 0.45 V. Beyond that, the peak of CDAC is pronounced in the range between 0.2 V and 0.6 V, whereas the SEI formation in AC spans much wider, between 0 V and 0.45 V. These results show that carbon dots in CDAC promote the electrolyte's decomposition, consistent with the result in Fig. 4. In addition, the oxidation peak and reduction peak of CDAC are symmetrical in shape, while those of AC do not. This suggests that CDAC demonstrates lower polarization and quicker reaction rates in comparison to AC [39].

Galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) tests were performed to measure sodium ions diffusion coefficients (Fig. S18 and Fig. 6 b-c). In the first sodiation process of CDAC (Fig. 6 b), the ionic diffusion coefficients gradually increase in the voltage range of 0.8 V to 0.4 V, then decrease again between 0.4 and 0.2 V, finally fluctuate in the voltage range of 0.2–0 V. During the charging process (Fig. S19), the diffusion coefficients decline first and then rise. It is clear that the diffusion coefficients of CDAC are larger than those of AC, indicating that the diffusion kinetics of the CDAC electrode is enhanced.

To obtain crucial insights on whether the inorganic-rich SEI or the defects-rich bulk phase contributes the enhanced diffusion behavior, in situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed during the first cycle (Fig. S20). According to Barsoukov’s model [40], Na+ diffuses in SEI and undergoes a charge transfer process at the interphase, which can be quantified by the impedance in the high frequency region (R SEI) and intermediate frequency region (R ct) (Fig. 6 d-e). It shows that R SEI of both electrodes tends to increase slightly as the reaction progresses, but the values of CDAC are lower than those of AC throughout the process. Likewise, the charge transfer resistances (R ct) of CDAC are significantly reduced as well, especially within the voltage range of 0.4 V to 0.7 V. A temperature-dependent electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) performed from −10 °C to 30 °C was further analyzed (Fig. 6 f) [41]. Both samples show sharp decreases of R ct and R SEI with the increase of temperature, but it is obvious that the impedance of CDAC is consistently lower over the entire temperature range. The activation energy for ion diffusion in the SEI (E SEI) and charge transfer (E ct) in the bulk phase were determined by the Arrhenius law, as shown in Fig. 6 g-h [42]. The calculated activation energy of CDAC are 71.3 KJ mol−1 (E SEI) and 65.9 KJ mol−1 (E ct) respectively, both are lower than AC (77.7 KJ mol−1 and 83.3 KJ mol−1). The above results confirm that the CDAC electrode enables a more kinetically efficient (de)sodiation process, not only in the SEI but in the bulk phase of materials.

In summary, the electrode/electrolyte interphase of a low-cost asphalt-derived carbon was engineered by introducing carbon dots. The expanded adsorption/bonding sites for sodium ions provided by defects/functional groups in carbon dots greatly improve the specific capacity, achieving a high slope-dominated capacity of 361 mA h g−1. In addition, the carbonyl groups introduced by carbon dots effectively enhance the adsorption and dissociation of anion (PF6 -), forming a localized high concentration of electrolyte at the interphase and thus tailoring the composition of SEI. The engineered interphase featured with more inorganic species such as NaF and Na2CO3 ultimately leads to a significant improvement of the reaction kinetics. Furthermore, it is demonstrated that surface defects are the main sources of slope capacity, but these defects result in low initial Coulombic efficiency and irreversible capacity. Based the above, it can be concluded that interfacial properties of disordered carbons, such as the degree of defects, functional groups, and electron escape ability, play a crucial role in deciphering their capacities and reaction kinetics, alongside the structural properties within the bulk phase. This study may provide some inspiration for developing advanced carbon materials and in-depth understanding of interphase behavior on sodium storage kinetics.

Haifeng Ding: Formal analysis. Liang Yue: Investigation, Formal analysis. Lixin Bai: Formal analysis. Zhiyong Xiong: Formal analysis. Yuruo Qi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Maowen Xu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the support from Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2023NSFSC1129), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22005251, No. 22179109), Central University Research Funding Talent Introduction Program (NO. SWU-KR22002).

ce:section.ce:para: Yi Zhang., Liang Yue., and Yuruo Qi., conceived and designed the experiment. Yi Zhang., prepared samples and carried out the main experiments. Yi Zhang., and Yue Liang., examined the electrochemical performance. Yandong Ma., Jing Zhao., Haifeng Ding., Zhiyong Xiong., Lixin Bai., and Juan Chen., assisted the investigation. Yi Zhang., Yuruo Qi., and Maowen Xu., wrote and revised the paper. All authors have read and approved the final paper.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yi Zhang received his bachelor's degree from Southwest University in 2020. He is currently a Ph.D. student of Prof. Maowen Xu at Southwest University. His research interests focus on novel energy storage system and application of carbon-based materials in sodium storage.

Liang Yue received his bachelor's degree from Dongguan University of Technology in 2018, and his master's degree from Southwest University. He is currently a Ph.D. student at the University of Science and Technology of China. His research interests focus on novel energy storage systems.

Haifeng Ding received his bachelor’s degree from Qingdao University in 2021. He is currently a mater student at Southwest University. His research interests focus on electrochemical energy storage, mainly including sodium-ion carbon anodes.

Zhiyong Xiong received his bachelor’s degree from southwest university in 2022. He is currently a master student following Prof. Maowen Xu in Southwest University. His research interests focus on novel energy storage system and carbon-based materials application in sodium storage.

Lixin Bai received his Bachelor's degree from Chongqing Metropolitan College of Science and Technology. He is currently a master student of Prof. Maowen Xu at Southwest University. His research interests focus on novel energy storage systems.

Maowen Xu is a professor at the Faculty of Materials and Energy, Southwest University. He earned his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University. He worked as a postdoc toral scholar in Prof. J. B. Goodenough's group of the University of Texas at Austin from 2010–2013. His current research is focused on the design and synthesis of electrode materials for sodium-ion batteries and lithium/sodium–sulfur batteries.

YuRuo Qi is currently a researcher at the School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University. She received her bachelor's degree from Sichuan University in 2013 and Ph.D. degree in 2019 from Institute of Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research interest focuses on sodium-ion batteries and Room-temperature sodium-sulfur batteries.
